topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, instantly freezing my breath in my chest. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic of my mouse digging into my palms. It was a notification about a publication, a piece I had poured months of late nights into, and the author listed was entirely wrong. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like spilled ink. I reread the text, my eyes scanning the unfamiliar name, the unfamiliar voice. Panic started to bubble up, a tight, suffocating pressure behind my ribs. I needed to verify this immediately, but every muscle in my body felt stiff, refusing to obey the simple command to click. I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly, a silent scream trapped behind my teeth.

[story 2]

I sat bolt upright in my chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath me. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. The printed proof lay spread across my desk, the glaringly familiar headline mocking me from the page. Someone else had taken my words, my unique perspective, and draped them in another person's identity. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I pushed back from the desk, pacing the small confines of my office, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. "This can't be right," I muttered to the empty room, the sound thin and reedy. I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting someone to walk in and shatter the fragile reality I was currently inhabiting.

[story 3]

My fingers hovered over the 'reply' button, slick with nervous perspiration. The realization hit me like a physical blow: my contribution, the culmination of countless hours of solitary effort, belonged to a stranger. A deep, hollow ache settled in my core, making it hard to draw a full breath. I pressed my forehead against the cool glass of the monitor, trying to anchor myself. Every flicker of movement from the outside world seemed amplified, too loud, too sharp. I slowly reached for my phone, my movements deliberate and slow, as if moving through thick water. When I finally typed the initial query, my hands shook so violently the keys clattered against the keyboard, a desperate, clumsy attempt to regain control over the spiraling situation.
